brief.topic: who lives in my family — naming family members (mom, dad, grandma, brother, sister) and what each person does to help

passageRow.subject: social-studies
passageRow.title: Who Lives in My Family?
passageRow.body:
## Who Lives in My Family?

Families are special. Each person helps. Moms and dads take care of us. They cook food and keep us safe.

Brothers and sisters are fun. They play and share toys. They help us learn new things.

Grandmas and grandpas are wise. They tell stories and give hugs. Everyone in the family is important.

Families can be big or small. Every family is different. But every family is full of love.
passageRow.topic: who lives in my family — naming family members (mom, dad, grandma, brother, sister) and what each person does to help
passageRow.topicNotes: Focus on family roles and contributions.

questionRows.explanation: Families can be big or small.

brief.topic: what is in my neighborhood — house, school, park, store, fire station — and what each place is for

passageRow.subject: social-studies
passageRow.title: My Neighborhood
passageRow.body:
## My Neighborhood

A neighborhood has many places. Some places are for fun. We play at the park. We learn at school.

We live in a house. Our family is there. We eat and sleep there.

We shop at the store. We buy food and toys. Helpers work there.

The fire station is important. Firefighters help us. They keep us safe.

All these places make our neighborhood special.
passageRow.topic: what is in my neighborhood — house, school, park, store, fire station — and what each place is for
passageRow.topicNotes: Focus on common neighborhood places and their purposes.

questionRows.explanation: The passage mentions helpers work at the store.

questionRows.explanation: The passage says firefighters keep us safe.

questionRows.question: What makes the neighborhood special?
questionRows.explanation: The passage says all these places make the neighborhood special.

brief.topic: people who help us — firefighters, police officers, doctors, teachers — and one thing each one does

passageRow.subject: social-studies
passageRow.title: People Who Help Us
passageRow.body:
## People Who Help Us

Firefighters are brave. They put out fires. They help keep us safe. Firefighters drive big red trucks.

Police officers wear blue uniforms. They help us follow rules. Police officers keep our community safe.

Doctors help us feel better. They check our health. Doctors use tools like stethoscopes.

Teachers help us learn. They teach us new things. Teachers work in schools and classrooms.
passageRow.topic: people who help us — firefighters, police officers, doctors, teachers — and one thing each one does
passageRow.topicNotes: Describes key roles of community helpers for young students.

questionRows.subject: social-studies

questionRows.explanation: Teachers help us learn.

questionRows.subject: social-studies
questionRows.question: Who uses stethoscopes?
questionRows.explanation: Doctors use tools like stethoscopes.

questionRows.subject: social-studies
questionRows.question: Who helps keep us safe?
questionRows.explanation: Firefighters, police officers, and doctors help keep us safe.

brief.topic: the Texas flag is red, white, and blue with one big white star — that is why Texas is called the Lone Star State

passageRow.subject: social-studies
passageRow.title: The Texas Flag
passageRow.body:
## The Texas Flag

The Texas flag has three colors. It is red, white, and blue. There is one big white star on the flag.

The star is special. It is why Texas is called the Lone Star State. Lone means one. The star stands for Texas being strong and proud.

The flag is important to Texans. It shows Texas pride. People wave the flag at parades and events. It reminds us of our state.
passageRow.topic: the Texas flag is red, white, and blue with one big white star — that is why Texas is called the Lone Star State
passageRow.topicNotes: Introduce young students to the Texas flag and its symbolism.

questionRows.explanation: The passage states the flag has three colors: red, white, and blue.

questionRows.explanation: The passage mentions a big white star on the flag.

questionRows.explanation: The passage explains that 'Lone' means one star.

questionRows.explanation: The passage states the star stands for Texas being strong and proud.

brief.topic: the mockingbird is the Texas state bird — it can copy songs from other birds

passageRow.subject: social-studies
passageRow.title: The Mockingbird: Texas State Bird
passageRow.body:
## The Mockingbird: Texas State Bird

The mockingbird is special. It is the Texas state bird. This bird is gray and white. It has long tail feathers.

The mockingbird can sing many songs. It copies songs from other birds. It is like a music star! Mockingbirds are fun to watch and hear.

You can see mockingbirds in many places in Texas. They like to be in parks and gardens. Next time you see one, listen to its song!
passageRow.topic: the mockingbird is the Texas state bird — it can copy songs from other birds
passageRow.topicNotes: Focus on the mockingbird as the Texas state bird and its ability to mimic songs.

questionRows.explanation: The passage states the mockingbird is the Texas state bird.

questionRows.explanation: The passage describes the mockingbird as gray and white.

questionRows.explanation: The passage states they like to be in parks and gardens.

brief.topic: the bluebonnet is the Texas state flower — it grows in spring and turns hills blue

passageRow.subject: social-studies
passageRow.title: The Bluebonnet
passageRow.body:
## The Bluebonnet

The bluebonnet is the Texas state flower. It is very special. The flower grows in the spring.

Bluebonnets turn the hills blue. They look like a sea of blue. Many people love to see them. Families take pictures with the flowers.

The bluebonnet has a sweet smell. Bees like to visit them. The flower helps make Texas beautiful. It is a symbol of Texas pride.
passageRow.topic: the bluebonnet is the Texas state flower — it grows in spring and turns hills blue
passageRow.topicNotes: Bluebonnet as state flower and springtime bloom.

brief.topic: a week has seven days — Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday — and night and day make one full day

passageRow.subject: social-studies
passageRow.title: Days of the Week
passageRow.body:
## Days of the Week

A week has seven days. Each day has a name. The first day is Monday. Then comes Tuesday. Next is Wednesday and Thursday. Friday is the last school day. Saturday and Sunday are the weekend.

Each day has night and day. The sun shines during the day. At night, the moon and stars come out. We sleep at night. We play and learn during the day. Every day is special. What is your favorite day?
passageRow.topic: a week has seven days — Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday — and night and day make one full day
passageRow.topicNotes: Introduce days of the week and concept of day and night.

brief.topic: why our classroom has rules — to keep us safe and to help everyone learn — naming three classroom rules

passageRow.subject: social-studies
passageRow.title: Classroom Rules Help Us
passageRow.body:
## Classroom Rules Help Us

Our classroom has rules. Rules keep us safe. They help us learn. Everyone can enjoy school!

One rule is to listen. When the teacher talks, we listen. This helps us learn new things.

Another rule is to share. We share toys and books. Sharing helps us play together.

The last rule is to be kind. We use kind words. Kindness makes friends happy. 

Rules help everyone. We feel safe and learn better. We love our classroom!
passageRow.topic: why our classroom has rules — to keep us safe and to help everyone learn — naming three classroom rules
passageRow.topicNotes: Explains why classroom rules are important using simple examples.
